words.def: Positively charged subatomic particle in the nucleus
Neutral subatomic particle in the nucleus
Negatively charged subatomic particle orbiting the nucleus
Central core of an atom containing protons and neutrons
Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Number of protons in the nucleus of an atom
Total number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus
Weighted mean mass of an atom relative to 1/12 mass of carbon-12
Instrument used to measure the relative masses of isotopes
Energy required to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy to remove the first electron from a mole of gaseous atoms
Energies required to remove electrons one by one from a gaseous ion
Region of space where there is a high probability of finding an electron
Division of an electron shell (s, p, d, f)
Arrangement of electrons in orbitals around the nucleus
Fixed energy value that an electron in an atom can have
Pattern of lines produced when excited electrons fall to lower energy levels
Lowest energy state of an atom or electron
Higher energy state of an atom when an electron has absorbed energy
Spherical orbital that can hold up to 2 electrons
Dumbbell-shaped orbital that can hold up to 2 electrons
More complex orbital holding up to 2 electrons (5 per subshell)
Electrons fill the lowest available energy orbitals first
No two electrons in the same atom can have identical quantum numbers
Electrons occupy separate orbitals of equal energy before pairing
Energy change when a gaseous atom gains an electron
Quantum of electromagnetic radiation
Number of wave oscillations per second
Distance between successive crests of a wave
Point where lines in a spectrum become too close to distinguish, indicating ionisation
Sum of the relative atomic masses of all atoms in a molecule
Amount of substance containing 6.02×10²³ particles
Number of particles in one mole: 6.02×10²³ mol⁻¹
Mass of one mole of a substance in g mol⁻¹
Simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound
Actual number of each type of atom in a molecule
Quantitative relationship between reactants and products
Reactant that is completely used up first, limiting the amount of product
Reactant present in greater quantity than required by stoichiometry
Ratio of actual yield to theoretical yield, expressed as percentage
Volume occupied by one mole of gas (24 dm³ at RTP)
Amount of solute dissolved per unit volume of solution (mol dm⁻³)
Masses of reactants and products calculated from a balanced equation
Hypothetical gas that obeys pV = nRT under all conditions
Ideal gas equation relating pressure, volume, moles and temperature
Sum of relative atomic masses in a formula unit
Percentage of reactant atoms that end up in the desired product
Technique to determine concentration by reacting with a standard solution
Solution of accurately known concentration
Analytical technique based on volume measurements of solutions
Substance dissolved in a solvent to form a solution
Insoluble solid formed during a reaction in solution
Containing no water of crystallisation
Containing water of crystallisation
Water molecules incorporated into the crystal structure of a salt
Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions
Bond formed by sharing a pair of electrons between two atoms
Covalent bond where both electrons come from one atom (also: coordinate bond)
Attraction between positive metal ions and a sea of delocalised electrons
Ability of an atom to attract bonding electrons towards itself
Covalent bond with unequal sharing of electrons due to electronegativity difference
Uneven distribution of electron density along a bond
Persistent separation of charge in a polar molecule
Temporary dipole caused by momentary uneven electron distribution
Dipole created in a molecule by a nearby charge or dipole
Weak intermolecular attractions including London dispersion forces
Intermolecular forces between induced instantaneous dipoles
Strong intermolecular attraction between δ+ H and lone pair on N, O or F
Model predicting molecular shape from repulsion of electron pairs
Pair of non-bonding electrons on an atom
Angle between two adjacent bonds in a molecule
Molecular shape with four bonds at 109.5°
Molecular shape with three bonds at 120° in the same plane
Molecular shape with a bond angle of 180°
Molecular shape with two bonding pairs and two lone pairs
Molecular shape with three bonding pairs and one lone pair
Bond formed by head-on overlap of orbitals along the internuclear axis
Bond formed by sideways overlap of p orbitals above and below the axis
Spreading of electrons over more than two atoms in a structure
One of two or more Lewis structures representing the same molecule
Distance between the nuclei of two bonded atoms
Energy required to break one mole of bonds in gaseous molecules
Forces of attraction or repulsion between molecules
Forces within a molecule (e.g. covalent bonds)
Attractive force between permanent dipoles of adjacent molecules
Gas that obeys pV = nRT perfectly; molecules have no volume and no intermolecular forces
Gas that deviates from ideal behaviour due to molecular volume and intermolecular forces
Model explaining gas behaviour in terms of random motion of particles
At constant temperature, pressure is inversely proportional to volume
At constant pressure, volume is proportional to absolute temperature
Regular 3D arrangement of alternating positive and negative ions
Network solid held together by a continuous system of covalent bonds
Regular arrangement of positive metal ions in a sea of delocalised electrons
Molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces in a lattice
Allotrope of carbon with a giant covalent tetrahedral structure; very hard
Allotrope of carbon with layers of hexagonal rings; conducts electricity
Different structural forms of the same element
Spherical allotrope of carbon (e.g. C₆₀)
Number of nearest neighbours surrounding an ion or atom in a lattice
Smallest repeating unit of a crystal lattice
Crystal packing where atoms occupy corners and face centres of cube
Crystal packing where atom is at centre of a cube with 8 corner atoms
Ionic lattice where each ion is surrounded by 6 oppositely charged ions
Ionic lattice where each ion is surrounded by 8 oppositely charged ions
Direct change of state from solid to gas without passing through liquid
Temperature at which solid and liquid are in equilibrium
Temperature at which liquid and gas are in equilibrium at standard pressure
Pressure exerted by vapour in equilibrium with its liquid
Resistance to flow in a liquid
Tendency of a liquid surface to minimize its area
Heat energy change at constant pressure (ΔH)
Reaction that releases heat energy (ΔH negative)
Reaction that absorbs heat energy (ΔH positive)
298 K, 100 kPa, solutions at 1 mol dm⁻³
Enthalpy change when one mole of substance burns completely in oxygen under standard conditions
Enthalpy change when one mole of compound is formed from its elements under standard conditions
Enthalpy change of a reaction is independent of the route taken
Diagram showing alternative routes between reactants and products
Technique to measure heat energy changes in reactions
Energy needed to raise the temperature of 1 g of substance by 1 K
Energy required to break one mole of a specified bond in gaseous molecules
Average bond enthalpy from a range of compounds
Enthalpy change when one mole of ionic lattice forms from gaseous ions
Energy cycle used to calculate lattice energy via Hess's law
Enthalpy change when one mole of gaseous atoms forms from the element
Enthalpy change when one mole of gaseous atoms gains one mole of electrons
Enthalpy change when one mole of gaseous ions dissolves in excess water
Enthalpy change when one mole of substance dissolves in excess solvent
Measure of disorder or randomness in a system (S)
ΔG = ΔH – TΔS; reaction is spontaneous if ΔG is negative
Reaction that proceeds without continuous external energy input
Minimum energy required for a reaction to occur
Diagram showing enthalpy of reactants, transition state and products
Balanced equation showing enthalpy change for the reaction as written
State where forward and reverse reactions occur at equal rates
Reaction that can proceed in both forward and reverse directions
System at equilibrium responds to stress by shifting to oppose the change
Ratio of product to reactant concentrations at equilibrium
Ratio of product to reactant partial pressures at equilibrium
Pressure exerted by one gas in a mixture
Ratio of moles of one component to total moles in mixture
Proton donor in an acid-base reaction
Proton acceptor in an acid-base reaction
Acid and its conjugate base (or base and its conjugate acid) differing by one proton
Measure of hydrogen ion concentration: pH = –log[H⁺]
Equilibrium constant for partial dissociation of a weak acid
Equilibrium constant for partial dissociation of a weak base
Ionic product of water: [H⁺][OH⁻] = 10⁻¹⁴ at 298 K
Solubility product: equilibrium constant for a sparingly soluble salt
Solution that resists changes in pH when small amounts of acid or base are added
Acid that is only partially dissociated in solution
Acid that is completely dissociated in solution
Substance that can act as both a proton donor and acceptor
Reaction with water causing dissociation or decomposition
Point in titration where moles of acid equal moles of base
Weak acid/base that changes colour with pH
Reduction in solubility of a salt by adding a common ion
–log(Ka); lower pKa means stronger acid
Splitting of acid molecules into ions in solution
Change in concentration of reactant or product per unit time
Mathematical expression: rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ
Proportionality constant in the rate equation; dependent on temperature
Power to which concentration term is raised in the rate equation
Rate proportional to concentration of one reactant: rate = k[A]
Rate proportional to concentration squared: rate = k[A]²
Rate independent of reactant concentration
Time for concentration to halve; constant for first-order reactions
Minimum energy needed for reactant particles to react
k = Ae^(–Ea/RT); shows how k varies with temperature
Reactions occur when particles collide with sufficient energy and correct orientation

words.zh: 麦克斯韦-玻尔兹曼分布
words.def: Graph showing distribution of molecular energies in a gas
Substance that increases reaction rate without being consumed
Catalyst in the same phase as the reactants
Catalyst in a different phase from the reactants
Highest energy point along a reaction pathway
Step-by-step sequence of elementary reactions in an overall reaction
Slowest step in a reaction mechanism that determines the overall rate
Species formed in one step of a mechanism and consumed in a later step
Determining rate equation by measuring reaction rate at time zero
Expression relating concentration to time for a given order
Biological catalyst, a protein that speeds up metabolic reactions
Substance that decreases reaction rate
Collision with sufficient energy and correct orientation to result in reaction
Regular repeating trends in properties across a period
Horizontal row of elements in the periodic table
Vertical column of elements in the periodic table
d-block element with an incomplete d subshell in at least one oxidation state
Element whose outermost electrons are in d orbitals
Ability of transition metals to exist in multiple oxidation states
Metal ion bonded to ligands by coordinate bonds
Molecule or ion that donates a lone pair to a central metal ion
Number of ligands bonded to the central metal ion
Ligand that forms two coordinate bonds with the metal ion
Complex with a ring formed by a polydentate ligand
Reaction forming an insoluble solid from aqueous ions
Hypothetical charge on an atom if all bonds were ionic
Species that gains electrons and is itself reduced
Species that loses electrons and is itself oxidised
Reaction where the same element is both oxidised and reduced
Ability of a transition metal to act as a catalyst
Transition metal ion that absorbs visible light due to d-d electron transitions
Equation showing only oxidation or reduction part of a redox reaction
Replacement of one ligand by another in a complex ion
Variation in ionisation energies across a period or down a group
Half the distance between nuclei of two adjacent atoms
Radius of an ion in an ionic crystal
Electronegativity generally increases across a period and decreases down a group
Reduction in nuclear attraction on outer electrons by inner electron shells
Group 2 element (Be, Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba, Ra)
Group 2 metals become more reactive down the group
Breakdown of a compound by heating
Compound containing OH⁻ ions
Compound of an element with oxygen
Salt of carbonic acid containing CO₃²⁻ ions
Salt of sulfuric acid containing SO₄²⁻ ions
Group 2 hydroxide solubility increases; sulfate solubility decreases down the group
Reaction between an acid and a base to form salt and water
Ca(OH)₂; used in agriculture to neutralise acid soils
MgO; used as antacid and refractory material
BaSO₄; insoluble white precipitate used in barium meals
BaCl₂; reagent used to test for sulfate ions
Dilute aqueous calcium hydroxide; turns milky with CO₂
Rapid release of gas bubbles from a liquid during a reaction
Substance that neutralises acids; proton acceptor
Substance that can act as both acid and base
Solution in which water is the solvent
Equation showing only the ions that change during a reaction
Ion that does not participate in a reaction
Group 17 element (F, Cl, Br, I, At)
Anion formed when a halogen gains one electron (F⁻, Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻)
More reactive halogen displaces less reactive halide from solution
Chlorine in water acts as both oxidising and reducing agent
Ability to gain electrons; decreases down Group 17
AgNO₃ solution used to identify halide ions by colour of precipitate
AgCl; white precipitate, soluble in dilute NH₃
AgBr; cream precipitate, soluble in concentrated NH₃
AgI; yellow precipitate, insoluble in NH₃
Aqueous solution of Cl₂ containing HCl and HClO
Removal of colour by oxidation using Cl₂ or ClO⁻
ClO⁻; formed when Cl₂ reacts with cold dilute NaOH
HF, HCl, HBr, HI; acid strength increases down the group
H₂SO₄ reacts with halide salts; distinguishes Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻
Ability to donate electrons; halide reducing power increases down group
Killing microorganisms; Cl₂ used to sterilise water supplies
Organic compound containing a halogen atom
Halogen dissolved in water; shows characteristic colours
Compound formed between two different halogens
Adding acidified AgNO₃ solution to identify Cl⁻, Br⁻ or I⁻
Industrial synthesis of ammonia: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃
Industrial synthesis of sulfuric acid via SO₃
Biogeochemical cycle converting nitrogen between its various chemical forms
Conversion of atmospheric N₂ to compounds usable by living organisms
NH₃; alkaline gas; used in fertilisers and nitric acid production
HNO₃; strong acid manufactured from ammonia by Ostwald process
NO₂; brown gas; pollutant formed in combustion engines
NO; colourless gas that reacts with O₂ to form NO₂
Precipitation made acidic by dissolved SO₂ and NOₓ
SO₂; acidic gas released from burning fossil fuels; causes acid rain
SO₃; formed from SO₂ oxidation; reacts with water to form H₂SO₄
Device in vehicles that converts NOₓ and CO to N₂ and CO₂
Substance that removes water; concentrated H₂SO₄ is a strong dehydrating agent
V₂O₅; catalyst in the Contact process
Fe catalyst used in the Haber process
4NH₃ + 5O₂ → 4NO + 6H₂O; first step of Ostwald process
Chemical providing nutrients (N, P, K) for plant growth
Over-enrichment of water with nutrients causing excessive algae growth
Rhombic and monoclinic sulfur; both contain S₈ rings
Oleum; H₂SO₄ with dissolved SO₃; used in sulfonation
Series of compounds with the same functional group differing by CH₂
Atom or group responsible for the characteristic reactions of a compound
Compounds with the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements
Compounds with same bonding but different spatial arrangement of atoms
Cis/trans isomers arising from restricted rotation around a C=C bond
Non-superimposable mirror image molecules (enantiomers)
Carbon atom bonded to four different groups
One of a pair of optical isomers
Equal mixture of two enantiomers; optically inactive
Electron-pair acceptor; attacks electron-rich centres
Electron-pair donor; attacks electron-deficient centres
Species with an unpaired electron
Bond breaking where each atom gets one electron
Bond breaking where both electrons go to one atom
International systematic naming rules for organic compounds
Formula showing all bonds and atoms explicitly
Simplified formula showing carbon skeleton with non-C/H atoms
Simplest whole-number ratio of atoms
Number of rings or double bonds in a molecule
Symbol representing movement of an electron pair in a mechanism
Positively charged carbon intermediate in organic reactions
Negatively charged carbon intermediate
Unequal distribution of electrons in a bond due to electronegativity
Transmission of charge through sigma bonds
Saturated hydrocarbon with general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂
Unsaturated hydrocarbon containing a C=C double bond
Aromatic hydrocarbon containing a benzene ring
C₆H₆; aromatic compound with delocalised π electrons
Mechanism for alkane halogenation: initiation, propagation, termination
Mechanism for alkene reactions with electrophiles
Mechanism for benzene ring reactions with electrophiles
H adds to carbon with more H atoms in addition to HX across C=C
Breaking down of large alkanes into smaller alkenes and alkanes
Substitution of NO₂ group into benzene ring using HNO₃/H₂SO₄
Addition of alkyl group to benzene using RCl and AlCl₃
Addition of acyl group to benzene using RCOX and AlCl₃
Introduction of halogen atoms into a molecule
Polymer formed by addition reactions of alkene monomers
Small repeating unit that polymerises to form a polymer
Geometric isomer with same groups on the same side of C=C
Geometric isomer with same groups on opposite sides of C=C
Containing only single bonds between carbon atoms
Containing at least one double or triple bond
Complex mixture of hydrocarbons obtained from crude oil
Separation of crude oil into fractions based on boiling points
Alkenes decolourise brown Br₂ water by addition across C=C
Converting straight-chain alkanes to branched or aromatic hydrocarbons
Alkane in which one or more H atoms are replaced by halogen atoms
Reaction where nucleophile replaces a leaving group
Two-step substitution: rate depends on one species; forms carbocation intermediate
One-step substitution: rate depends on two species; backside attack
Removal of atoms from adjacent carbons to form a double bond
Two-step elimination through carbocation intermediate
One-step elimination with simultaneous bond breaking and formation
Halogenoalkane where the halogen-bearing carbon is bonded to one alkyl group
Halogenoalkane where the halogen-bearing carbon is bonded to two alkyl groups
Halogenoalkane where the halogen-bearing carbon is bonded to three alkyl groups
Group that departs with the electron pair during substitution
Chlorofluorocarbon; ozone-depleting refrigerant/propellant
Destruction of stratospheric ozone by radical chain reaction with Cl·
Reaction with water (or aqueous NaOH) to replace halogen with OH
Acidified AgNO₃ identifies halogenoalkane type by precipitate colour/rate
RI > RBr > RCl due to decreasing C–X bond strength
Walden inversion in SN2: product has opposite configuration to reactant
Formation of equal amounts of both enantiomers, as in SN1 at chiral centre
Organomagnesium halide RMgX; useful for C–C bond formation
Measure of nucleophile's ability to donate electron pair to electrophile
Organic compound containing –OH group bonded to a carbon chain
Compound with –OH group directly bonded to benzene ring
Alcohol where –OH carbon is bonded to one other carbon
Alcohol where –OH carbon is bonded to two other carbons
Alcohol where –OH carbon is bonded to three other carbons
Primary → aldehyde → carboxylic acid; secondary → ketone; tertiary: no reaction
K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄ used to oxidise alcohols; Cr₂O₇²⁻ → Cr³⁺ (orange→green)
Reaction of an alcohol with a carboxylic acid to form an ester and water
Elimination of water from an alcohol to form an alkene
Conversion of glucose to ethanol and CO₂ by yeast enzymes
ZnCl₂/HCl used to distinguish primary, secondary and tertiary alcohols
I₂/NaOH produces CHI₃ precipitate with methyl ketones or CH₃CH(OH)–
Phenol is weakly acidic; reacts with NaOH but not Na₂CO₃
Brightly coloured compound containing –N=N– group; made from diazonium coupling
ArN₂⁺ ion formed from primary aromatic amine and NaNO₂/HCl at 0°C
Diazonium ion reacts with phenol/amine to form azo dye
OH group activates benzene ring towards electrophilic substitution
RONa; formed when Na metal reacts with an alcohol
–OH groups form hydrogen bonds; raises boiling point significantly
Short-chain alcohols are miscible with water; solubility decreases with chain length
Compound with –CHO group; terminal carbonyl
Compound with C=O group flanked by two carbon atoms
Nucleophile attacks the δ+ carbon of a carbonyl group
2,4-DNPH; orange reagent forming orange/yellow precipitate with aldehydes and ketones
Ammoniacal AgNO₃; forms silver mirror with aldehydes but not ketones
Blue Cu²⁺ solution; gives red Cu₂O precipitate with aldehydes
Aldehydes are easily oxidised to carboxylic acids; ketones are not
NaBH₄ or LiAlH₄ reduces C=O to –OH
Product of addition of HCN to a carbonyl compound
CN⁻ attacks carbonyl carbon; H⁺ adds to O; forms hydroxynitrile
I₂/NaOH gives yellow CHI₃ precipitate with CH₃CO– or CH₃CHOH–
Reaction of carbonyl with amine derivative to form C=N and eliminate water
Addition of two alcohol molecules to an aldehyde in acidic conditions
Equilibrium between keto (C=O) and enol (C–OH) forms
Light vibrating in one plane; used in polarimetry to detect optical activity
Equal mixture of two enantiomers formed when C=O reacts with nucleophile
Used to identify carbonyl compounds via molecular ion and fragmentation
C=O stretch at ~1700 cm⁻¹ identifies carbonyl compounds
Used to determine structure of carbonyl compounds from chemical shifts
Reaction between two carbonyl compounds to form a β-hydroxy carbonyl compound
Loss of electrons; increase in oxidation number
Gain of electrons; decrease in oxidation number
Reaction involving transfer of electrons between species
EMF of a half-cell measured relative to standard hydrogen electrode
Electrode potential measured under standard conditions (E°)
Reference electrode with E° = 0 V; H₂/H⁺ at 1 atm, 1 mol dm⁻³, 298 K
Device that converts chemical energy into electrical energy via redox reaction
Electromotive force; maximum potential difference produced by a cell
E°cell = E°cathode – E°anode; positive value indicates feasible reaction
Electrode where reduction occurs (gains electrons)
Electrode where oxidation occurs (loses electrons)
Use of electric current to drive a non-spontaneous redox reaction
Mass of product in electrolysis is proportional to charge passed
Charge carried by one mole of electrons: 96500 C mol⁻¹
One electrode and its electrolyte in an electrochemical cell
Allows ion flow between half-cells to maintain electrical neutrality
Shorthand representation: anode | solution || solution | cathode
Reaction is feasible if Ecell > 0 (or ΔG < 0)
Element simultaneously oxidised and reduced in the same reaction
Shows how electrode potential varies with concentration
Oxidation of metals by environment; iron rusting is an electrochemical process
Electrochemical cell generating electricity from fuel oxidation (e.g. H₂/O₂)
Cell in which electrolysis is carried out using external power supply
Compound containing –COOH group
Compound formed from alcohol + carboxylic acid/acyl chloride; contains –COO–
RCOX; very reactive acylating agent; reacts vigorously with water
RCO–O–OCR; less reactive acylating agent than acyl chloride
Compound with one or more –NH₂ groups; basic due to lone pair on N
Compound with –CONH₂ group; formed from carboxylic acid + amine
Compound with both –NH₂ and –COOH groups; building block of proteins
Internal salt form of amino acid at isoelectric point
–CO–NH– linkage between amino acids in a peptide/protein
Polymer formed with elimination of small molecule (e.g. H₂O or HCl)
Condensation polymer formed between diol and dicarboxylic acid
Condensation polymer formed between diamine and dicarboxylic acid (e.g. nylon)
Alkaline hydrolysis of ester to produce alcohol and carboxylate salt
Ester + H₂O → acid + alcohol (acid catalysed); ester + NaOH → salt + alcohol
Mechanism for reactions of acyl chlorides and anhydrides with nucleophiles
Amines are weak bases; lone pair on N accepts protons
pH at which amino acid has equal + and – charges; net charge = 0
Amine with one alkyl/aryl group on N: RNH₂
Amine with two alkyl/aryl groups on N: R₂NH
Amide + Br₂/NaOH → primary amine with one fewer carbon atom
Alkene monomers join together by breaking the C=C double bond
Monomers join with elimination of small molecules (H₂O, HCl)
Large molecule made of many repeating monomer units
Smallest recurring structural unit in a polymer chain
Number of monomer units in a polymer chain
Polymer that softens on heating and can be remoulded; chains are not cross-linked
Polymer that does not soften on heating; heavily cross-linked
Covalent bonds between polymer chains increasing rigidity and melting point
Polyamide condensation polymer used in textiles and engineering
Polyester condensation polymer used in fibres and bottles
Addition polymer of ethene; LDPE (branched) and HDPE (linear)
Addition polymer of propene; isotactic (regular) and atactic (random)
Polymer broken down by microorganisms; reduces environmental impact
Non-biodegradable polymers accumulating in environment
Breaking down polymer into monomers; used for recycling
Polymer made from two or more different monomers
Aromatic polyamide with exceptional strength; used in bulletproof vests
Cross-linking of rubber chains with sulfur to improve strength and elasticity
Loss of electrons or increase in oxidation state
Gain of electrons or decrease in oxidation state
A reaction involving simultaneous oxidation and reduction
A substance that oxidises another substance and is itself reduced
A substance that reduces another substance and is itself oxidised
The hypothetical charge on an atom if all bonds were ionic
A number assigned to an element in a compound representing its degree of oxidation
An equation showing either the oxidation or reduction process separately
The movement of electrons from one species to another in a redox reaction
A redox reaction where an element is simultaneously oxidised and reduced
An equation showing only the ions and molecules that actually take part in a reaction
An ion that is present in a reaction mixture but does not take part in the reaction
Rules used to assign oxidation numbers to atoms in compounds
MnO₄⁻, a strong oxidising agent used in titrations
Cr₂O₇²⁻, a strong oxidising agent that turns from orange to green when reduced
A quantitative analytical technique using a standard solution to determine the concentration of another solution
A solution of accurately known concentration
The point in a titration where the moles of titrant are stoichiometrically equivalent to the moles of analyte
The enthalpy change when one mole of ionic solid is formed from its gaseous ions
An application of Hess's law used to calculate lattice energy
The enthalpy change when one mole of gaseous atoms is formed from an element in its standard state
The energy required to remove one electron from each atom in one mole of gaseous atoms
The enthalpy change when one mole of gaseous atoms gains one electron
The enthalpy change when one electron is added to each atom in one mole of gaseous atoms
The enthalpy change when one electron is added to each ion in one mole of gaseous singly-charged negative ions
The enthalpy change when one mole of a compound is formed from its elements in their standard states
The total enthalpy change in a reaction is independent of the route taken
The radius of an ion in an ionic crystal
The charge per unit volume of an ion; higher charge density leads to stronger ionic interactions
The distortion of the electron cloud of an anion by a cation
The ease with which an ion's electron cloud is distorted
Lattice energy calculated assuming purely ionic bonding using Coulomb's law
Lattice energy derived from the Born-Haber cycle using experimental data
The enthalpy change when one mole of gaseous ions is completely surrounded by water molecules
The enthalpy change when one mole of solute dissolves in a large amount of solvent
The maximum amount of a solute that dissolves in a given amount of solvent at a given temperature
A proton donor in an acid-base reaction
A proton acceptor in an acid-base reaction
Two species related by the gain or loss of a single proton
A measure of hydrogen ion concentration; pH = -log[H⁺]
An acid that fully dissociates in aqueous solution
An acid that only partially dissociates in aqueous solution
Ka, the equilibrium constant for the dissociation of a weak acid
Equilibrium constant expression for weak acid dissociation: Ka = [H⁺][A⁻]/[HA]
The negative logarithm of Ka; pKa = -log Ka
A solution that resists changes in pH when small amounts of acid or base are added
A buffer with pH below 7, made from a weak acid and its conjugate base
A buffer with pH above 7, made from a weak base and its conjugate acid
Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 1×10⁻¹⁴ mol²dm⁻⁶ at 25°C
The equilibrium constant for the ionisation of water; Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻]
The reaction between an acid and a base to form salt and water
The point in a titration where acid and base have reacted in stoichiometric amounts
A weak acid whose undissociated and dissociated forms have different colours
Ksp, the equilibrium constant for the dissolution of a sparingly soluble ionic compound
The decrease in solubility of an ionic compound when a common ion is added to the solution
A species that can act as both an acid and a base
The change in concentration of a reactant or product per unit time
An expression relating the rate of reaction to the concentrations of reactants: rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ
k in the rate equation; its value depends on temperature
The power to which a concentration is raised in the rate equation
The sum of the individual orders of all reactants in the rate equation
Reaction order where rate is independent of concentration of that reactant
Reaction order where rate is directly proportional to concentration of that reactant
Reaction order where rate is proportional to the square of the concentration
The time taken for the concentration of a reactant to fall to half its initial value
k = Ae^(-Ea/RT), relating rate constant to temperature and activation energy
The minimum energy required for a reaction to occur
A (pre-exponential factor) in the Arrhenius equation, related to frequency of collisions with correct orientation
The pre-exponential factor A in the Arrhenius equation
A method of determining reaction orders by measuring rates at the start of the reaction
The slowest step in a reaction mechanism that determines the overall rate
A step-by-step description of how a reaction proceeds at the molecular level
A species formed during a reaction that is consumed in a later step
A substance that increases reaction rate without being consumed overall
A catalyst that is in the same phase as the reactants
A catalyst that is in a different phase from the reactants
A measure of the disorder or randomness of a system; symbol S
The entropy of one mole of a substance under standard conditions (298 K, 100 kPa)
ΔS = S(products) - S(reactants)
G = H - TS; a thermodynamic function that predicts whether a reaction is feasible
ΔG = ΔH - TΔS; used to calculate whether a reaction is spontaneous
ΔG⊖, the Gibbs free energy change under standard conditions
A reaction that proceeds without any external input of energy; occurs when ΔG < 0
Whether a reaction can occur spontaneously; a reaction is feasible when ΔG ≤ 0
The total entropy of an isolated system always increases in a spontaneous process
ΔS_total = ΔS_system + ΔS_surroundings; positive for spontaneous processes
ΔS_surr = -ΔH/T; the entropy change of the surroundings due to heat transfer
298 K (25°C) and 100 kPa (1 bar); used as reference for thermodynamic data
The degree of randomness or lack of order in a system, related to entropy
The number of ways energy can be distributed among particles in a system; S = k·lnW
The relationship between ΔG and temperature via the TΔS term in ΔG = ΔH - TΔS
An element that has an incomplete d subshell in at least one of its stable ions
An element in which the highest energy electron is in a d orbital
The ability of transition metals to form ions with different oxidation states
A metal ion surrounded by ligands that donate electron pairs to the metal
A molecule or ion that donates a lone pair of electrons to a central metal ion
The number of coordinate bonds formed between the central metal ion and its ligands
A covalent bond where both electrons are donated by one atom (coordinate bond)
A ligand that forms one coordinate bond with the central metal ion (e.g. H₂O, NH₃, Cl⁻)
A ligand that forms two coordinate bonds with the central metal ion (e.g. en, ethanedioate)
A ligand that forms more than two coordinate bonds (e.g. EDTA forms 6 bonds)
A complex formed when a polydentate ligand bonds to a metal ion in a ring structure
The extra stability of chelate complexes relative to those with monodentate ligands
A reaction in which one or more ligands in a complex are replaced by other ligands
Transition metal ions that absorb visible light due to d-d electron transitions
The promotion of an electron between d orbitals of different energy, which absorbs light and produces colour
The ability of transition metals to act as catalysts by using variable oxidation states
Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid; a hexadentate ligand that forms very stable complexes
An iron-containing protein in red blood cells that transports oxygen using Fe²⁺ complex
The formation of an insoluble solid from a solution; used to identify transition metal ions
A hydroxide that reacts with both acids and bases (e.g. Al(OH)₃, Cr(OH)₃, Zn(OH)₂)
A cyclic hydrocarbon C₆H₆ with a delocalised π electron system; the simplest aromatic compound
A hydrocarbon containing a benzene ring (aromatic hydrocarbon)
A compound containing a benzene ring or similar aromatic ring system
Electrons that are spread over several atoms rather than localised between two atoms
A reaction where an electrophile replaces a hydrogen atom on the benzene ring
The introduction of a nitro group (–NO₂) into a compound using a nitrating mixture
A mixture of concentrated HNO₃ and concentrated H₂SO₄ used for nitration
NO₂⁺, the electrophile formed in nitration reactions
Electrophilic substitution using an acyl or alkyl halide with a Lewis acid catalyst
Introduction of an acyl group (RCO–) onto the benzene ring using Friedel-Crafts conditions
Introduction of an alkyl group onto the benzene ring using Friedel-Crafts conditions
C₆H₅OH; benzene with a hydroxyl group attached, which activates the ring
C₆H₅NH₂ (aniline); benzene with an amino group, also activates the ring
The influence of a substituent on the position of further electrophilic substitution (ortho/para or meta)
A substituent that increases the rate of electrophilic substitution by donating electron density to the ring
A substituent that decreases the rate of electrophilic substitution by withdrawing electron density
The early structure of benzene with alternating single and double bonds proposed by Kekulé
A molecule best described as a mixture of two or more contributing structures
An organic compound derived from ammonia by replacing H with alkyl or aryl groups (–NH₂, –NHR, –NR₂)
An amine with one alkyl or aryl group attached to nitrogen (RNH₂)
An amine with two alkyl or aryl groups attached to nitrogen (R₂NH)
An amine with three alkyl or aryl groups attached to nitrogen (R₃N)
Amines are bases because nitrogen has a lone pair that can accept a proton
A compound containing the –CONH₂ group; formed by reaction of carboxylic acid with amine
The –CO–NH– linkage formed between amino acids; an amide bond
A compound with both amino (–NH₂) and carboxyl (–COOH) groups; building blocks of proteins
A molecule with both a positive and negative charge at different sites; form of amino acids at neutral pH
The pH at which an amino acid exists primarily as a zwitterion with zero net charge
Ar–N₂⁺ X⁻; formed by diazotisation of primary aromatic amines at low temperature
The reaction of a primary aromatic amine with NaNO₂/HCl at 0–5°C to form a diazonium salt
Reaction of a diazonium salt with a coupling component (phenol or amine) to form an azo dye
A coloured compound containing the –N=N– group, made by coupling reactions
C₆H₅NO₂ → C₆H₅NH₂ (phenylamine) using Sn/HCl followed by NaOH
A reaction where a nucleophile replaces a leaving group
A method for preparing primary amines from phthalimide and haloalkanes
Conversion of a primary amide to a primary amine with one fewer carbon using Br₂/NaOH
Working backwards from a target molecule to identify suitable starting materials and reagents
The sequence of reactions and reagents used to convert a starting material to a target molecule
Transforming one functional group into another during a synthesis
A group temporarily added to prevent an unwanted reaction at that site during synthesis
The preference for one reaction outcome (regiochemical or stereochemical) over another
The amount of product obtained as a percentage of the theoretical maximum
actual yield / theoretical yield × 100%
The fraction of the mass of reactants converted to desired product: (MW product / MW all products) × 100%
Chemistry that aims to minimise environmental impact through efficient, sustainable reactions
An unwanted or secondary product formed in a chemical reaction
A synthesis requiring several sequential reactions to reach the target molecule
The arrangement of carbon atoms forming the backbone of an organic molecule
A reaction that increases the length of the carbon chain in a molecule
Temperature, pressure, catalyst, solvent and other factors affecting a reaction
The process of removing impurities from a product (e.g. recrystallisation, distillation)
A purification technique where a solid is dissolved and allowed to crystallise again to remove impurities
A technique for identifying compounds by measuring the mass-to-charge ratios of ions
The peak at the highest m/z in a mass spectrum corresponding to M⁺, the molecular ion
The most intense peak in a mass spectrum, set to 100% relative abundance
The characteristic way a molecule breaks apart in a mass spectrometer, used for identification
A technique that identifies functional groups by measuring absorption of infrared radiation
The process by which radiation is taken up by a substance, causing molecular vibrations
The number of wavelengths per centimetre (cm⁻¹); used to indicate position of IR absorption peaks
The region of an IR spectrum below 1500 cm⁻¹ that is unique to each compound
A technique using the behaviour of atomic nuclei in a magnetic field to determine molecular structure
The position of an NMR peak measured in ppm relative to TMS (δ = 0)
Tetramethylsilane, Si(CH₃)₄; the reference standard for NMR chemical shift (δ = 0 ppm)
The area under an NMR peak, proportional to the number of equivalent protons
Interaction between neighbouring nuclear spins that causes peak splitting in NMR
A ¹H NMR peak is split into (n+1) lines by n equivalent neighbouring protons
An NMR peak split into 2 lines due to 1 neighbouring proton
An NMR peak split into 3 lines due to 2 neighbouring equivalent protons
An NMR peak split into 4 lines due to 3 neighbouring equivalent protons
Protons in the same chemical environment that give a single NMR signal
NMR spectroscopy using ¹H nuclei; the most common type used in organic structure determination
NMR spectroscopy using ¹³C nuclei; each unique carbon environment gives one peak
